title: Charmed Life - Chapter 11 (Fragment 4)
text:
“Are you feeling well, Eric?” Mr. Saunders asked several times.

“Oh, yes,” Cat replied each time. He was fairly sure that having your mind in three places at once did not count as illness, much as it felt like it.

“Play soldiers?” Roger suggested after lessons.

Cat would have liked to, but he dared not leave Janet on her own. “I’ve got to do something,” he said.

“With Gwendolen. I know,” Roger said wearily. “Anyone would think you were her left leg, or something.”

Cat felt hurt. The annoying thing was that he knew Janet could have done without her left leg more easily than she could have done without him. As he hurried after Janet to Gwendolen’s room, he wished heartily it was really Gwendolen he was hurrying after.

Inside the room, Janet was feverishly collecting things: Gwendolen’s spell books, the ornaments on the mantelpiece, the gold-backed brush and hand mirror off the dressing table, the jar on the bedside table, and half the towels from the bathroom.

“What are you doing?” said Cat.

“Finding things we can sell. Is there anything you can bear to spare from your room?” said Janet. “Don’t look like that. I know it amounts to stealing, but I get so desperate when I think of that horrible Mr. Bisto going to Chrestomanci that I don’t care anymore.” She went to the wardrobe and rattled the clothes along the rail. “There’s an awfully good coat in here.”

“You’ll need that on Sunday if it turns cold,” Cat said drearily. “I’ll go and see what I’ve got—only promise me to stay here until I come back.”

“Sho’ ting,” said Janet. “I daren’t move widdout you, bwana. But hurry up.”

There were fewer things in Cat’s room, but he collected what he could find, and added the great sponge from the bathroom. He felt like a criminal. Janet and he wrapped their finds in two towels and crept downstairs with their chinking bundles, expecting someone to discover them any minute.

“I feel like a thief with the swag,” Janet whispered. “Someone’s going to shine a searchlight any second, and then the police will close in. Are there police here?”

“Yes,” said Cat. “Do shut up.”

But, as usual, there was no one about near the private door. They crept down the shiny passage and peeped outside. The space by the rhododendrons was empty. They crept out towards them. Trees that would hide Mr. Baslam would hide them and their loot.

They were three steps outside the door when a massed choir burst into song. Janet and Cat nearly jumped out of their skins. “We belong to Chrestomanci Castle! We belong to Chrestomanci Castle!” thundered forty voices. Some were deep, some were shrill, but all were very loud. They made a shattering noise. It took them a second or so to realize that the voices were coming from their bundles.

“Creeping antimacassars!” said Janet.

They turned around and ran for the door again, with the forty voices bawling in their ears.

Miss Bessemer opened the door. She stood tall and narrow and purple, waiting for them to come through it. There was nothing Janet and Cat could do but scuttle guiltily past her into the passage, where they put their suddenly silent bundles down on the floor and steeled themselves for trouble.

vocabulary.definition: To be equivalent to or to have the same effect as something else.
vocabulary.example: I know it amounts to stealing, but I don’t care.

vocabulary.definition: A small cloth placed over the back of a chair to protect it from hair oil or dirt.
vocabulary.example: Creeping antimacassars!

vocabulary.definition: In a clumsy or uncomfortable manner.
vocabulary.example: He moved awkwardly in the tight space.

vocabulary.definition: Objects tied or wrapped up together.
vocabulary.example: They put their bundles on the floor.

vocabulary.definition: Making a light ringing or clinking sound.
vocabulary.example: Their chinking bundles made noise as they crept down stairs.

vocabulary.definition: An outer garment, typically sleeveless, worn over indoor clothing.
vocabulary.example: A coat is an outerwear similar to a cloak.

vocabulary.definition: Gathering together objects or items.
vocabulary.example: Janet was feverishly collecting things.

vocabulary.definition: A person who has committed a crime.
vocabulary.example: He felt like a criminal while wrapping the towels.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that shows a hopeless sense that a situation is impossible to deal with.
vocabulary.example: She felt desperately anxious about the future.

vocabulary.definition: In a boring or dull manner that makes one feel sad or tired.
vocabulary.example: He spoke drearily about the weather.

vocabulary.definition: A piece of bedroom furniture with a mirror used for putting on makeup or dressing.
vocabulary.example: The hand mirror was on the dressing table.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that shows intense excitement, activity, or anxiety.
vocabulary.example: Janet was feverishly collecting things.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that shows a sense of guilt or remorse.
vocabulary.example: They scuttled guiltily through the passage.

vocabulary.definition: To move or act with speed.
vocabulary.example: He hurried after Janet.

vocabulary.definition: Goods or money obtained illegally or by force.
vocabulary.example: They hid their loot behind the trees.

vocabulary.definition: A structure of wood, marble, or stone above and around a fireplace.
vocabulary.example: The ornaments stood on the mantelpiece.

vocabulary.definition: Gathered together in a large group.
vocabulary.example: A massed choir burst into song.

vocabulary.definition: Limited in width or extent.
vocabulary.example: She stood tall and narrow and purple.

vocabulary.definition: Decorative objects used to adorn something.
vocabulary.example: She took the ornaments off the mantelpiece.

vocabulary.definition: The act of allowing something to happen.
vocabulary.example: “Only promise me to stay,” said Cat.

vocabulary.definition: To move hurriedly with short quick steps.
vocabulary.example: They scuttled guiltily past her.

vocabulary.definition: A very powerful light used to search for something in the dark.
vocabulary.example: He feared a searchlight would expose them.

vocabulary.definition: Extremely shocking or upsetting; loud and overwhelming.
vocabulary.example: They made a shattering noise.

vocabulary.definition: High-pitched and piercing in sound.
vocabulary.example: Some were deep, some were shrill voices.

vocabulary.definition: To mentally prepare oneself to face a difficult situation.
vocabulary.example: They steeled themselves for trouble.

vocabulary.definition: Goods or valuables, often stolen.
vocabulary.example: Janet whispered she felt like a thief with the swag.

vocabulary.definition: Material used to cover or enclose something.
vocabulary.example: He wrapped their finds in towels.
